 - List what vendor defaults are changed 
 - That unnecessary default accounts (including accounts used by operating systems, security software, applications, systems, POS terminals, SNMP, etc.) are removed or disabled before a system is installed on the network. 
 - List what vendor defaults are removed 
 - Identify industry-accepted hardening standard(s) that the system configuration standards is consistent with.  
 - List industry-accepted hardening standard(s) 
 - That system configuration standards are updated as new vulnerability issues are identified.  
 - Indicate that this is done (must be stated in policy/process) 
 - That system configuration standards are applied when new systems are configured, and verified as being in place before a system is installed on the network  
 - Indicate that this is done (must be stated in policy/process) 
 - That all vendor-supplied defaults are changed, and unnecessary default accounts are eliminated 
 - Indicate that this is done (must be stated in policy/process) 
 - That only one primary function per server is implemented (to prevent functions that require different security levels from co-existing on the same server) 
 - The demonstrated primary function should reconcile with the CDE inventory provided during the pre-assessment phase 
 - That only necessary services, protocols, daemons, etc., are enabled as required for the function of the system; 
 - List enabled necessary services, protocols, daemons, etc. 
 - Implementing additional security features for any required services, protocols or daemons that are considered to be insecure 
 - List additional security features for any insecure services, protocols or daemons, etc. that are required 
 - Configuring system security parameters to prevent misuse 
 - List implemented security parameters to prevent misuse of systems 
 - Removing all unnecessary functionality, such as scripts, drivers, features, subsystems, file systems, and unnecessary web servers 
 - List removed default functionality (scripts, drivers, features, subsystems, file systems, and unnecessary web services) 
 - That common security parameter settings are applied  
 - List applied common security parameter settings 
 - That enabled functions are documented  
 - The enabled functions should support what is indicated in the CDE inventory provided during the pre-assessment phase
(ROC|1.2.1.a, 1.3.1.a, 1.3.2.a, 1.4.1.a, 1.5.1.a)

evidence_requests.name: Incident response plan and procedures
evidence_requests.text:
Provide documented incident response plan and related procedures used by the organization to respond immediately to a system breach, with defined processes as follows from 

-Create the incident response plan to be implemented in the event of a system breach.
-Test the plan at least annually.
-Designate specific personnel to be available on a 24/7 basis to respond to alerts:
- 24/7 incident monitoring
- 24/7 incident response
-Provide appropriate training to staff with security breach response responsibilities.
-Include alerts from security monitoring systems, including but not limited to intrusion-detection, intrusion-prevention, firewalls, and file integrity monitoring systems.
-  Develop a process to modify and evolve the incident response plan according to lessons learned and to incorporate industry developments.
(ROC|12.10.1)

evidence_requests.name: Multiple methods of communicating awareness and educating personnel
evidence_requests.text:
Examples of different means of communicating awareness to personnel regarding information security, phishing, and protecting account data.
(ROC|12.6.3.b)

evidence_requests.name: Listing of all users with privileged access to CDE systems, applications, and data
evidence_requests.text:
Full listing of all privileged users to the assessor; the QSA will then select a subset for inclusion within the assessment report.
(ROC|7.2.3.b)

evidence_requests.name: Logs are captured for all types of events
evidence_requests.text:
Provide evidence showing that logs are created for each of the below:
-All security events
-Logs of all system components that store, process, or transmit CHD and/or SAD
-Logs of all critical system components
-Logs of all servers and system components that perform security functions (for example, firewalls, intrusion-detection systems/intrusion-prevention systems (IDS/IPS), authentication servers, e-commerce redirection servers, etc.)
(ROC|10.2.1.1,  10.2.1.2, 10.2.1.3, 10.2.1.4, 10.2.1.5, 10.2.1.6, 10.2.1.7, 5.3.2.c, 5.3.3.c, 6.4.2, 8.2.1.b)

evidence_requests.name: Configuration settings for the intrusion detection or prevention systems
evidence_requests.text:
As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to obtain and review the configuration settings for the intrusion detection and/or prevention systems.  Required configuration data to be provided include:
-Evidence of IDS/IPS rules
-Evidence of update frequency and mechanism and current status
-Evidence of alert and notification settings
(ROC|11.5.1.a, 11.5.1.c, 11.5.1.1)

evidence_requests.name: (Sample) Logged audit events for data repositories
evidence_requests.text:
-All individual access to cardholder data.
- All actions taken by any individual with root or administrative privileges.
-Access to all audit trails.
-Invalid logical access attempts.
-Use of and changes to identification and authentication mechanisms, including:
-All elevation of privileges.
-All changes, additions, or deletions to any account with root or administrative privileges.
-Initialization of audit logs.
-Stopping or pausing of audit logs.
-Creation and deletion of system level objects.
(ROC|10.2.1.1,  10.2.1.2, 10.2.1.3, 10.2.1.4, 10.2.1.5, 10.2.1.6, 10.2.1.7)

evidence_requests.name: (Sample) Logged audit events for network infrastructure
evidence_requests.text:
-All individual access to cardholder data.
- All actions taken by any individual with root or administrative privileges.
-Access to all audit trails.
-Invalid logical access attempts.
-Use of and changes to identification and authentication mechanisms, including:
-All elevation of privileges.
-All changes, additions, or deletions to any account with root or administrative privileges.
-Initialization of audit logs.
-Stopping or pausing of audit logs.
-  Creation and deletion of system level objects.
(ROC|10.2.1.1,  10.2.1.2, 10.2.1.3, 10.2.1.4, 10.2.1.5, 10.2.1.6, 10.2.1.7)

evidence_requests.name: (Sample) Logged audit events for server technologies
evidence_requests.text:
-All individual access to cardholder data.
- All actions taken by any individual with root or administrative privileges.
-Access to all audit trails.
-Invalid logical access attempts.
-Use of and changes to identification and authentication mechanisms, including:
-All elevation of privileges.
-All changes, additions, or deletions to any account with root or administrative privileges.
-Initialization of audit logs.
-Stopping or pausing of audit logs.
-  Creation and deletion of system level objects.
(ROC|10.2.1.1,  10.2.1.2, 10.2.1.3, 10.2.1.4, 10.2.1.5, 10.2.1.6, 10.2.1.7)

evidence_requests.name: (Sample) Logged audit events for virtual technologies
evidence_requests.text:
-All individual access to cardholder data.
-  All actions taken by any individual with root or administrative privileges.
-Access to all audit trails.
-Invalid logical access attempts.
-Use of and changes to identification and authentication mechanisms, including:
-All elevation of privileges.
-All changes, additions, or deletions to any account with root or administrative privileges.
-Initialization of audit logs.
-Stopping or pausing of audit logs.
-  Creation and deletion of system level objects.
(ROC|10.2.1.1,  10.2.1.2, 10.2.1.3, 10.2.1.4, 10.2.1.5, 10.2.1.6, 10.2.1.7)

evidence_requests.name: (Sample) Logged audit events for workstations
evidence_requests.text:
-All individual access to cardholder data.
-  All actions taken by any individual with root or administrative privileges.
-Access to all audit trails.
-Invalid logical access attempts.
-Use of and changes to identification and authentication mechanisms, including:
-All elevation of privileges.
-All changes, additions, or deletions to any account with root or administrative privileges.
-Initialization of audit logs.
-Stopping or pausing of audit logs.
-  Creation and deletion of system level objects.
(ROC|10.2.1.1,  10.2.1.2, 10.2.1.3, 10.2.1.4, 10.2.1.5, 10.2.1.6, 10.2.1.7)

evidence_requests.name: All CDE systems exist within the organization's log management systems
evidence_requests.text:
As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to confirm that all CDE systems and devices are connected to and reporting audit logs to the organization's log management system(s).  Common evidence provided includes a full listing export from the SIEM tool(s), showing by hostname, what devices/systems are reporting logs to which SIEM.
(ROC|10.2.1)

evidence_requests.name: Reporting of change detection and FIM events for in scope components
evidence_requests.text:
Managed FIM/change detection platform of system settings for each technology considered in scope of the CDE, to include, the monitored files and parameter settings, and the results of the monitoring which identify activity and its handling by authorized personnel.
(ROC|10.3.4)

evidence_requests.name: In-scope inventory of systems and devices for reporting
evidence_requests.text:
Current inventory of all systems, devices that operate within and/or are connected to/impact the security of the cardholder environment.
(ROC|12.5.1.a)

evidence_requests.name: Vendor documentation for system components' access control management.
evidence_requests.text:
Vendor documentation (files or links to vendor online documentation) for all access control systems in the CDE and systems connected to the CDE or that provide security services such as FIM, Anti-Malware, Logging, etc.
(ROC|7.3.1, 7.3.2, 7.3.3)

evidence_requests.name: Internal penetration test results
evidence_requests.text:
Penetration test results from most recent internal penetration testing is performed as follows: 
- Testing is performed per the defined methodology  
- At least annually 
- After any significant changes to the environment. 
- By a qualified internal resource or qualified external third party.
(ROC|11.4.2.a)

evidence_requests.name: Descriptions of roles and responsibilities activities for Requirement 1 are documented and assigned
evidence_requests.text:
Roles and responsibilities may be documented within policies and procedures or maintained within separate documents. As part of communicating roles and responsibilities, entities can consider having personnel acknowledge their acceptance and understanding of their assigned roles and responsibilities. A method to document roles and responsibilities is a responsibility assignment matrix that includes who is responsible, accountable, consulted, and informed (also called a RACI matrix). evidence_requests.name: Policy and procedures for Requirement 1
evidence_requests.text:
Purpose 
This Requirement is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout the Requirement. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in the requirement and sub-requirements, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated. 

Definitions
Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.
 
The documents must have been reviewed since the last assessment and contain updates or an entry in a document control table that shows the review or update. File dates and submit dates can not be used to validate this. The same document may be used for all requirements.
(ROC|1.1.1)

evidence_requests.name: Policy and procedures for Requirement 9
evidence_requests.text:
Purpose 
This Requirement is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout the Requirement. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in the requirement and sub-requirements, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated. 

Definitions
Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.

Policy and Procedure Documents should address all of the following topics:
-	Authorize and manage physical access of personnel to the CDE are defined
-	Authorizing and managing visitor access to the CDE
-	Protecting cardholder data include controls for physically securing all media
-	Physically securing offline media backups with cardholder data in a secure location.
-	Reviewing the security of the offline media backup location(s) with cardholder data at least once every 12 months
-	Storage location’s security is reviewed at least once every 12 months
-	Classifying media with cardholder data in accordance with the sensitivity of the data.
-	Securing media sent outside the facility
-	Ensure that media moved outside the facility is approved by management
-	Maintain electronic media inventory logs
-	Conduct inventories of electronic media with cardholder data at least once every 12 months
-	Destroy hard-copy media with cardholder data when no longer needed for business or legal reasons
-	Destroy electronic media when no longer needed for business or legal reasons 
-	Periodic inspections of POI device surfaces to detect tampering and unauthorized substitution
The documents must have been reviewed since the last assessment and contain updates or an entry in a document control table that shows the review or update. File dates and submit dates can not be used to validate this. This request may be fulfilled with one or multiple documents.
(ROC|9.1.1, 9.3.1.a, 9.3.2.a, 9.4.1, 9.4.1.1.a, 9.4.1.2.a, 9.4.1.2.b, 9.4.2.a, 9.4.3.a, 9.4.4.a, 9.4.5.a, 9.4.5.1.a, 9.4.6.a, 9.4.7.a, 9.5.1, 9.5.1.2.a)

evidence_requests.name: Policy and procedures for Requirement 10
evidence_requests.text:
Purpose 
This Requirement is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout the Requirement. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in the requirement and sub-requirements, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated. 

Definitions
Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.

Policy and procedure documentation should address the following topics:
-	Processes are defined for reviewing logs from components described in 10.4.1 at least once daily
-	Processes are defined for reviewing logs of all other system components periodically
-	Processes are defined for addressing exceptions and anomalies identified during the review process
-	Audit Log Retention policies are defined
-	Procedures for retaining audit log history for at least 12 months, with at least the most recent three months immediately available online.
-	For Service Provider assessments, processes are defined for the prompt detection and addressing of failures of critical security control systems
-	Prompt detection and addressing of failures of critical security control systems
-	Processes are defined and implemented to respond to a failure of any critical security control  ystem
The documents must have been reviewed since the last assessment and contain updates or an entry in a document control table that shows the review or update. File dates and submit dates can not be used to validate this. One single document or multiple documents may be needed for this request.
(ROC|10.1.1, 10.4.1.a, 10.4.2.a, 10.4.3.a, 10.5.1.a)

-	Processes are defined to detect and identify both authorized and unauthorized wireless access points

(ROC|11.1.1, 11.2.1.a, 11.2.1.b, 11.3.1.1.b, 11.4.5.a, 11.4.5.a, 11.4.6.a, 11.4.7)

evidence_requests.name: (Sample) Configuration settings for network security devices
evidence_requests.text:
Including: Firewalls, Routers, Switches, Load Balancers, Web Application Firewalls, Web Filters, and Web Proxy. Configuration exports or screenshots are acceptable but must detail the entire configuration. See instructions in the collection scripts folder of the PCI Assessment Resources in Project Files.
(ROC|1.2.2.b, 1.2.2.c, 6.5.3.b, 8.4.1.a)

evidence_requests.name: Disclosure protections for IP addresses
evidence_requests.text:
Restricting the disclosure of internal, private, and local IP addresses is useful to prevent a hacker from obtaining knowledge of these IP addresses and using that information to access the network. 

Good Practice Methods used to meet the intent of this requirement may vary, depending on the specific networking technology being used. For example, the controls used to meet this requirement may be different for IPv4 networks than for IPv6 networks. 

Examples 
Methods to obscure IP addressing may include, but are not limited to: 
-IPv4 Network Address Translation (NAT). 
-Placing system components behind proxy servers/NSCs. 
-Removal or filtering of route advertisements for internal networks that use registered addressing. 
-Internal use of RFC 1918 (IPv4) or use IPv6 privacy extension (RFC 4941) when initiating outgoing sessions to the internet.
(ROC|1.4.5.b)

evidence_requests.name: Prevent copying of PAN when remotely connected to the CDE
evidence_requests.text:
Examine documented policies and procedures and documented evidence for technical controls that prevent copy and/or relocation of PAN when using remote-access technologies onto local hard drives or removable electronic media to verify the following: 
- Technical controls prevent all personnel not specifically authorized from copying and/or relocating PAN. 
- A list of personnel with permission to copy and/or relocate PAN is maintained, together with the documented, explicit authorization and legitimate, defined business need.
(ROC|3.4.2.a)

evidence_requests.name: Cryptographic suites and protocols documentation
evidence_requests.text:
The inventory of trusted keys helps the entity keep track of the algorithms, protocols, key strength, key custodians, and key expiry dates. This enables the entity to respond quickly to vulnerabilities discovered in encryption software, certificates, and cryptographic algorithms. For certificates, the inventory should include the issuing CA and certification expiration date.
(ROC|2.2.7.a, 2.3.1.c, 2.3.2, 3.3.2, 3.3.3.b, 3.5.1.b, 3.5.1.1.c, 3.5.1.2.a, 3.5.1.3.a, 3.6.1.1, 3.6.1.2.b, 4.2.1.b, 4.2.1.1.b, 4.2.1.2, 4.2.2.b, 8.3.2.b, 8.3.2.c, 8.6.2.b, 10.6.2, 12.3.3, 12.5.2.b, A3.2.1)

evidence_requests.name: TRAs for the periodic evaluation of system components at risk for malware
evidence_requests.text:
Entities determine the optimum period to undertake the evaluation based on criteria such as the complexity of each entity’s environment and the number of types of systems that are required to be evaluated.
(ROC|5.3.2.1.a, 12.3.1)

evidence_requests.name: Periodic evaluation of system components not at risk for malware
evidence_requests.text:
Entities determine the optimum period to undertake the evaluation based on criteria such as the complexity of each entity’s environment and the number of types of systems that are required to be evaluated.
(ROC|5.2.3.1.b)

evidence_requests.name: Listing of components identified as not at risk of malware
evidence_requests.text:
Listing of components identified during the periodic analysis  as not at risk of malware.
(ROC|5.2.3.c)

evidence_requests.name: Periodic malware scans
evidence_requests.text:
Entities can determine the optimum period to undertake periodic scans based on their own assessment of the risks posed to their environments.
(ROC|5.3.2.1.b)

evidence_requests.name: Inventory of payment page scripts
evidence_requests.text:
Scripts loaded and executed in the payment page can have their functionality altered without the entity’s knowledge and can also have the functionality to load additional external scripts (for example, advertising and tracking, tag management systems). 
Such seemingly harmless scripts can be used by potential attackers to upload malicious scripts that can read and exfiltrate cardholder data from the consumer browser. 
Ensuring that the functionality of all such scripts is understood to be necessary for the operation of the payment page minimizes the number of scripts that could be tampered with. 
Ensuring that scripts have been explicitly authorized reduces the probability of unnecessary scripts being added to the payment page without appropriate management approval. 
Using techniques to prevent tampering with the script will minimize the probability of the script being modified to carry out unauthorized behavior, such as skimming the cardholder data from the payment page.
(ROC|6.4.3.b)

evidence_requests.name: Listing of accounts used by installed in-house or off-the-shelf applications
evidence_requests.text:
List of system and application accounts for all CDE systems. The assessor will use this information during interviews and observations to sample the use of default credentials.
(ROC|6.5.6.c)

evidence_requests.name: Periodic reviews of user accounts
evidence_requests.text:
Regular review of access rights helps to detect excessive access rights remaining after user job responsibilities change, system functions change, or other modifications. If excessive user rights are not revoked in due time, they may be used by malicious users for unauthorized access. 

This review provides another opportunity to ensure that accounts for all terminated users have been removed (if any were missed at the time of termination), as well as to ensure that any third parties that no longer need access have had their access terminated.

When a user transfers into a new role or a new department, typically the privileges and access associated with their former role are no longer required. Continued access to privileges or functions that are no longer required may introduce the risk of misuse or errors. Therefore, when responsibilities change, processes that revalidate access help to ensure user access is appropriate for the user’s new responsibilities.

Entities can consider implementing a regular, repeatable process for conducting reviews of access rights, and assigning “data owners” that are responsible for managing and monitoring access to data related to their job function and that also ensure user access remains current and appropriate.
(ROC|7.2.4.b)

evidence_requests.name: TRAs for the periodic review of application and system accounts and privileges
evidence_requests.text:
Targeted risk assessment results for the analysis of the need to perform review of access rights helps to detect excessive access rights remaining after system functions change, or other application or system modifications occur. If excessive rights are not removed when no longer needed, they may be used by malicious users for unauthorized access.
(ROC|7.2.5.1.b, 12.3.1)

evidence_requests.name: Authorizations for account lifecycle maintenance
evidence_requests.text:
It is imperative that the lifecycle of a user ID (additions, deletions, and modifications) is controlled so that only authorized accounts can perform functions, actions are auditable, and privileges are limited to only what is required. Attackers often compromise an existing account and then escalate the privileges of that account to perform unauthorized acts, or they may create new IDs to continue their activity in the background. It is essential to detect and respond when user accounts are created or changed outside the normal change process or without corresponding authorization. Authorizations may be in the form of service requests, HRIS actions, Email, or other workflow management tools.
(ROC|8.2.4)

evidence_requests.name: Deactivation validation for users whose employment has been terminated
evidence_requests.text:
If an employee or third party/vendor has left the company and still has access to the network via their user account, unnecessary or malicious access to cardholder data could occur—either by the former employee or by a malicious user who exploits the old and/or unused account. Evidence should show the deactivation in system user lists or that user lists no longer contain the user account for both local and remote access.
(ROC|8.2.5.a)

evidence_requests.name: Third-party account access, status, and approvals
evidence_requests.text:
Documented approval (for example, in writing or electronically) that shows  authorization for  third-party access and privileges are known and authorized by management, and that their access is justified.
(ROC|8.2.7)

evidence_requests.name: Packet captures of data transmissions from CDE systems
evidence_requests.text:
Packet captures that include the process of logging in to CDE systems to demonstrate that  devices and applications do not transmit unencrypted, readable authentication factors (such as passwords and passphrases) across the network.
(ROC|8.3.2.c)

evidence_requests.name: Accounts used for both interactive login and for system or application access
evidence_requests.text:
Like individual user accounts, system and application accounts require accountability and strict management to ensure they are used only for the intended purpose and are not misused.
Attackers often compromise system or application accounts to gain access to cardholder data.
Where possible, configure system and application accounts to disallow interactive login to prevent unauthorized individuals from logging in and using the account with its associated system privileges, and to limit the machines and devices on which the account can be used.
System or application accounts are those accounts that execute processes or perform tasks on a computer system or application and are not typically accounts that an individual logs into. These accounts usually have elevated privileges that are required to perform specialized tasks or functions.
Interactive login is the ability for a person to log into a system or application account in the same manner as a normal user account. Using system and application accounts this way means there is no accountability and traceability of actions taken by the user.
(ROC|8.6.1, 11.3.1.2.c)

evidence_requests.name: TRAs for change frequency and complexity of passwords or passphrases used for interactive login to application and system accounts
evidence_requests.text:
Provide targeted risk analysis results for the change frequency and complexity for passwords/passphrases used for interactive login to application and system accounts to verify the risk analysis was performed in accordance with all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1 and addresses:
-The frequency defined for periodic changes to application and system passwords/passphrases.
-The complexity defined for passwords/passphrases and appropriateness of the complexity relative to the frequency of changes.
(ROC|8.6.3.b, 12.3.1)

evidence_requests.name: Physical access control list
evidence_requests.text:
List of personnel including name and badge number if applicable who have access to sensitive areas where CHD is stored, processed, or transmitted.
(ROC|9.3.1.1.a)

evidence_requests.name: Electronic media inventory for CDE data
evidence_requests.text:
Inventories of electronic media with cardholder data that are conducted at least once every 12 months.
(ROC|9.4.5.1.b)

evidence_requests.name: TRAs for the frequency of periodic POI device inspections
evidence_requests.text:
The frequency of periodic POI device inspections and the type of inspections performed is defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.
(ROC|9.5.1.2.1.a, 12.3.1)

evidence_requests.name: Records of POI device inspections
evidence_requests.text:
Provided documented results of periodic device inspections which verify that the frequency and type of POI device inspections performed match what is defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis conducted for this requirement.
(ROC|9.5.1.2.1.b)

evidence_requests.name: TRAs for the frequency of periodic log reviews of system components
evidence_requests.text:
The frequency of periodic POI log reviews for components not included in 10.4.1 is defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.
(ROC|10.4.2.1.a, 12.3.1)

evidence_requests.name: Evidence audit logs are retained for at least 12 months with three months immediately available for analysis
evidence_requests.text:
Audit log samples which verify that logs are retained for at least 12 months.
(ROC|10.5.1.b)

evidence_requests.name: (Sample) Change logs to time synchronization settings
evidence_requests.text:
Provide log samples that show changes to time synchronization settings.
(ROC|10.6.3.b)

evidence_requests.name: Critical security control failures
evidence_requests.text:
Records that show critical security controls system failures are identified, the duration of the failure, the remediation, and root cause determination and resolution.
(ROC|10.7.3.b)

evidence_requests.name: Inventory of systems that cannot accept credentials for authenticated scanning
evidence_requests.text:
Documentation of systems that are unable to accept credentials for authenticated scanning.
(ROC|11.3.1.2.d)

evidence_requests.name: Annual hardware and software review
evidence_requests.text:
Hardware and software technologies in use are reviewed at least once every 12 months, including at least the following: 
-Analysis that the technologies continue to receive security fixes from vendors promptly. 
-Analysis that the technologies continue to support (and do not preclude) the entity’s PCI DSS compliance. 
-Documentation of any industry announcements or trends related to a technology, such as when a vendor has announced “end of life” plans for a technology. 
-Documentation of a plan, approved by senior management, to remediate outdated technologies, including those for which vendors have announced “end of life” plans.
(ROC|12.3.4)

evidence_requests.name: PCI DSS compliance program charter
evidence_requests.text:
Responsibility is established by executive management for the protection of cardholder data and a PCI DSS compliance program to include: 
-Overall accountability for maintaining PCI DSS compliance. 
-Defining a charter for a PCI DSS compliance program and communication to executive management.
(ROC|12.4.1)

evidence_requests.name: Quarterly review processes
evidence_requests.text:
Reviews are performed at least once every three months to confirm that personnel are performing their tasks in accordance with all security policies and operational procedures. Reviews are performed by personnel other than those responsible for performing the given task and include, but are not limited to, the following tasks: 
-Daily log reviews. 
-Configuration reviews for network security controls. 
-Applying configuration standards to new systems. 
-Responding to security alerts. 
-Change management processes.
(ROC|12.4.2.a, 12.4.2.b, 12.4.2.1)

evidence_requests.name: Annual scope reviews
evidence_requests.text:
PCI DSS scope is documented and confirmed by the entity at least once every 12 months and upon significant change to the in-scope environment. At a minimum, the scoping validation includes: 
-Identifying all data flows for the various payment stages (for example, authorization, capture settlement, chargebacks, and refunds) and acceptance channels (for example, card-present, card-not-present, and e-commerce). 
-Updating all data-flow diagrams per Requirement 1.2.4. 
-Identifying all locations where account data is stored, processed, and transmitted, including but not limited to: 1) any locations outside of the currently defined CDE, 2) applications that process CHD, 3) transmissions between systems and networks, and 4) file backups. 
-Identifying all system components in the CDE, connected to the CDE, or that could impact security of the CDE. 
-Identifying all segmentation controls in use and the environment(s) from which the CDE is segmented, including justification for environments being out of scope. 
-Identifying all connections from third-party entities with access to the CDE. 
-Confirming that all identified data flows, account data, system components, segmentation controls, and connections from third parties with access to the CDE are included in scope.
(ROC|ES, 12.5.2.a, 12.5.2.b)

evidence_requests.name: Policy and procedures for evaluating scope after a significant change
evidence_requests.text:
Significant changes to organizational structure result in a documented (internal) review of the impact to PCI DSS scope and applicability of controls, with results communicated to executive management.

Provide policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined such that a significant change to organizational structure results in documented review of the impact to PCI DSS scope and applicability of controls.
(ROC|12.5.3.a)

evidence_requests.name: Scope evaluations and executive communications after significant changes
evidence_requests.text:
Significant changes to organizational structure result in a documented (internal) review of the impact to PCI DSS scope and applicability of controls, with results communicated to executive management.

Provide formal internal documentation that shows scope was evaluated after a significant change and the results were communicated to executive management.
(ROC|12.5.3.b)

evidence_requests.name: Security awareness training content
evidence_requests.text:
The threat environment and an entity’s defenses are not static. As such, the security awareness program materials must be updated as frequently as needed to ensure that the education received by personnel is up to date and represents the current threat environment.

An effective security awareness program should include examples of phishing emails and periodic testing to determine the prevalence of personnel reporting such attacks. Training material an entity can consider for this topic include: 
- How to identify phishing and other social engineering attacks. 
- How to react to suspected phishing and social engineering. 
- Where and how to report suspected phishing and social engineering activity.

Different methods that can be used to provide security awareness and education include posters, letters, web-based training, in-person training, team meetings, and incentives. Personnel acknowledgments may be recorded in writing or electronically.
(ROC|12.6.2, 12.6.3.b, 12.6.3.d, 12.6.3.d, 12.6.3.1, 12.6.3.2)

evidence_requests.name: Agreements with third-party service providers
evidence_requests.text:
Provide copies of written agreements with Third-Party Service Providers that show: 
-Written agreements are maintained with all TPSPs with which account data is shared or that could affect the security of the CDE. 
-Written agreements include acknowledgments from TPSPs that they are responsible for the security of account data the TPSPs possess or otherwise store, process, or transmit on behalf of the entity, or to the extent that they could impact the security of the entity’s CDE.
(ROC|12.8.2.b)

evidence_requests.name: Vendor engagement processes
evidence_requests.text:
Provide formal internal documentation that shows an established process is implemented for engaging TPSPs, including proper due diligence prior to engagement.

Specific due-diligence processes and goals will vary for each organization. Elements that should be considered include the provider’s reporting practices, breach-notification and incident response procedures, details of how PCI DSS responsibilities are assigned between each party, how the TPSP validates their PCI DSS compliance and what evidence they provide.
(ROC|12.8.3.b)

evidence_requests.name: Responsibilities are managed by each TPSP and shared with the entity
evidence_requests.text:
Information is maintained about which PCI DSS requirements are managed by each TPSP, which are managed by the entity, and any that are shared between the TPSP and the entity.

Entities can document these responsibilities via a matrix that identifies all applicable PCI DSS requirements and indicates for each requirement whether the entity or TPSP is responsible for meeting that requirement or whether it is a shared responsibility. This type of document is often referred to as a responsibility matrix.

It is also important for entities to understand whether any TPSPs have “nested” relationships with other TPSPs, meaning the primary TPSP contracts with another TPSP(s) for the purposes of providing a service. It is important to understand whether the primary TPSP is relying on the secondary TPSP(s) to achieve overall compliance of a service, and how the primary TPSP is monitoring performance of the service and the PCI DSS compliance status of the secondary TPSP(s). Note that it is the responsibility of the primary TPSP to manage and monitor any secondary TPSPs.

Refer to Information Supplement: Third-Party Security Assurance for a sample responsibility matrix template.
(ROC|12.8.5.b)

evidence_requests.name: TPSP PCI DSS responsibilities
evidence_requests.text:
Policy and procedure documentation that details how TPSP entities acknowledge in writing to customers that they are responsible for the security of account data the TPSP possesses or otherwise stores, processes, or transmits on behalf of the customer, or to the extent that they could impact the security of the customer’s CDE. Additionally this documentation should include how TPSPs fulfill their customers requests related to their customer's compliance documentation related to meeting Requirement 12.8.4 and 12.8.5.
(ROC|12.9.2)

evidence_requests.name: Contract template for agreement with clients
evidence_requests.text:
Templates that are used for  agreements with your clients that demonstrate how the entity provides a consistent level of understanding with customers about their applicable PCI DSS responsibilities. The agreement should in clued an acknowledgement by the  entity regarding  their commitment to maintaining proper security of account data that it obtains from its clients.
(ROC|12.9.1)

evidence_requests.name: TRAs for the frequency of training for incident response personnel
evidence_requests.text:
Targeted risk analysis results for the change frequency and complexity for the frequency of training for incident response personnels to verify the risk analysis was performed in accordance with all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1. 

Each entity’s environment and incident response plan are different and the approach will depend on a number of factors, including the size and complexity of the entity, the degree of change in the environment, the size of the incident response team, and the turnover in personnel. Performing a risk analysis will allow the entity to determine the optimum frequency for training personnel with incident response responsibilities.
(ROC|12.10.4.1.a, 12.3.1)

evidence_requests.name: Periodic training of incident response personnel is performed
evidence_requests.text:
Formal documentation that shows periodic training of incident response personnel is performed per the frequency identified in the targeted risk analysis.
(ROC|12.10.4.1.b)

evidence_requests.name: Policy and procedures for Requirement 7
evidence_requests.text:
Purpose 
This Requirement is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout the Requirement. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in the requirement and sub-requirements, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated. 

Definitions
Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.

The documents must have been reviewed since the last assessment and contain updates or an entry in a document control table that shows the review or update. File dates and submit dates can not be used to validate this. The same document may be used for all requirements.
(ROC|7.1.1, 7.1.2, 7.2.1, 7.2.3, 7.2.4, 7.2.5, 7.2.5.1, 7.2.6, 7.3.1, 7.3.2, 7.3.3)

evidence_requests.name: External penetration testing results
evidence_requests.text:
Penetration test results from most recent external penetration test including evidence of the following: 
- Testing is performed per the defined methodology 
- At least annually 
- After any significant changes to the environment. 
- By a qualified internal resource or qualified external third party
(ROC|11.4.3.a)

evidence_requests.name: Logical separation controls for multi-tenant environments
evidence_requests.text:
Documentation of the policies, procedures, and controls that are used to create logical separation in multi-tenant environments including:
- The provider cannot access its customers' environments without authorization
- Customers cannot access the provider's environment without authorization

evidence_requests.name: Listing of customer accounts in the multi-tenant environment
evidence_requests.text: A listing of customer accounts which includes at least  the UserID, and organization name.

evidence_requests.name: Documentation of terminals that use early SSL or TLS
evidence_requests.text: Documentation of the POI devices in use that have SSL or early TLS enabled and that they are not susceptible to known exploits.

evidence_requests.name: Risk mitigation plan for use of SSL or early TLS
evidence_requests.text:
Risk Mitigation and Migration Plan for devices that use SSL or early TLS  that includes:
- Description of usage, including what data is being transmitted, types and number of systems that use and/or support SSL/early TLS, and type of environment
- Risk-assessment results and risk-reduction controls in place
- Description of processes to monitor for new vulnerabilities associated with SSL/early TLS
- Description of change control processes that are implemented to ensure SSL/early TLS is not implemented into new environments
- Overview of migration project plan to replace SSL/early TLS at a future date

evidence_requests.name: Early TLS vendor documentation
evidence_requests.text: Documentation that Service Providers provide to customers covering configuration and risk management for customer's devices that use SSL or early TLS.

evidence_requests.name: P2PE Implementation Manual (PIM)
evidence_requests.text:
Documented P2PE implementation manual (PIM) for supported POIs to identify the organization's procedures for making annual updates to the material, as well as, communicating it externally/internally to applicable affected solution customers and partners.
(ROC|ES)

evidence_requests.name: TRAs for sub-requirements met through the customized approach
evidence_requests.text:
Documentation of the PCI focused targeted risk analysis (TRA). This risk analysis is required for all service providers and merchants that are pursuing the customized approach for a set of applicable controls.
(ROC|12.3.2)

evidence_requests.name: TRAs for the periodic review of system components not at risk for malware
evidence_requests.text:
Entities determine the optimum period to undertake the targeted risk analysis (TRA) based on criteria such as the complexity of each entity’s environment and the number of types of systems that are required to be evaluated.
(ROC|5.2.3.1.b, 12.3.1)

evidence_requests.name: TRAs for addressing all other vulnerabilities from internal scans
evidence_requests.text:
Entities determine the targeted risk analysis' (TRA) optimum period to address all other vulnerabilities (those not ranked as high-risk or critical) that appeared in the scan results.
(ROC|11.3.1.1.a, 12.3.1)

evidence_requests.name: TRAs for the frequency of change and tamper-detection mechanism payment page evaluations
evidence_requests.text:
Entities determine the targeted risk analysis' (TRA) optimum period for the change and tamper-detection mechanism to evaluate its payment pages.
(ROC|11.6.1.c, 12.3.1)

evidence_requests.name: Results from documented review of system and application accounts
evidence_requests.text:
Entities determine the optimum period for reviewing the access privileges of application and system accounts.
(ROC|7.2.5.1.c)

evidence_requests.name: Results from documented review of logs from other system components
evidence_requests.text:
Entities determine the optimum period for reviewing logs of all other system components not defined in sub-requirement 10.4.1.
(ROC|10.4.2.1.b)

evidence_requests.name: Listing of all current user, group, and shared accounts
evidence_requests.text:
User account listing includes name, title, role, hire date, and last logon date.

Group/shared account listing includes name/ID, business justification, and teams/roles with access to this account.
(ROC|8.2.2.a, 8.2.5.a, 8.2.6)

evidence_requests.name: Exception list for PAN copying or relocation
evidence_requests.text:
List of personnel exceptions needs to include the each user's name; title; who authorized this privilege; and a legitimate, documented business need.
(ROC|3.4.2.a)

evidence_requests.name: Scan results for removable media
evidence_requests.text:
Provide scan results demonstrating that the tool:
- Performs automatic scans of when the media is inserted, connected, or logically mounted,
OR
- Performs continuous behavioral analysis of systems or processes when the media is inserted, connected, or logically mounted.
(ROC|5.3.3.c)

evidence_requests.name: Anti-phishing mechanism
evidence_requests.text:
Provide details about the anti-phishing mechanism that demonstrates how it protects end users from phishing attacks. 
(ROC|5.4.1.c)

evidence_requests.name: (Sample) Personnel acknowledgements of information security roles and responsibilities
evidence_requests.text:
Ensure that information security roles and responsibilities are defined, and that user acknowledgements are tracked. Tracking must include the name of the employee and date of the acknowledgement. This acknowledgement can be standalone or included as part of the acknowledgement process for security awareness training.
(ROC|12.1.3.c, 12.6.3.d)

evidence_requests.name: (Sample) Deployed POI devices
evidence_requests.text:
Documented sample listing of locations and payment capture terminals to ensure that POIs in those location(s) match the records.
(ROC| 9.5.1.1.a)
